item.title: 10 years Cake Day - Have some of the recent woodworking stuff i have saved to show my dad
item.description:
My dad is a woodworker for the past 45+ years https://imgur.com/gallery/some-other-things-dad-made-GmJfT , so I show him some interesting woodworking stuff from here from time to time that i think he migt like.
He has a lot of antique hand tools, and a few older machines (tho not this old), but it is always interesting to see older methods such as the above for making shingles.
Or just big saws, who doesnt like a big saw :D
He has also done work like this for nightclubs and pubs in ireland and around the world, in plaster, concrete, resin, as well as wood
We have a bunch of wood that is covered in shipworm holes that he has used recently (the wood was given to him by a client specifically to make something from it), so it was interesting to see where it comes from. The wood looks great, the food less so lol
He has had all the tool brands over the years, so it was good to see who would win
He has also made a lot of jigs over the years for specific jobs
Anyway, 10 years since ive had an account, thanks for all the fun :)
item.dc:creator: IRCBrink

item.title: Stuff my husband sends me
item.description:
Not a lesbian, but in this house, we listen and we don't judge.
Dude is a great feminist ally!
Dreams. Goals.
Adulting gets zero stars.
Life imitating art. This is our 16 year old at the Video Game History Museum
Getting this for our new house!
Creating a TED Talk about the perils of not teaching social studies with fidelity anymore. This bolsters my argument!
He was schooling our 19 year old on "Good music"
Correlation does not equal causation.
Because I have ADHD
Thanks 2024!!

item.title: I Have Hyperlactation Syndrome
item.description: Elisabeth Anderson-Sierra has become well-known in Beaverton, Oregon, for her decision to donate her breast milk to families in need. The mother of two has hyperlactation syndrome, causing her to produce 1.75 gallons of milk a day, with 5,000 ounces stored in her freezer. She holds the world record for the largest individual donation of breast milk — in what she describes as her “labor of love.”
item.dc:creator: 60SecondDocs

item.title: Life has been rough lately. (VENT)
item.description: Hi, imgur. I was debating on whether or not I should post this. I usually just lurk around here and haven't posted in a while. But I kind of needed to vent a bit, and instead of bothering my family and friends with my problems, I'm going to vent here a bit, comfortable in the knowledge that this will just die in usersub. I lost my job last month. It was a decently paying job in retail management, which I have 14 years of experience in. I had worked for the company in previous years, left for a while, and then returned to the company. I miss it, but it wasn't meant to be, I suppose. I've applied for unemployment as soon as I could, and my application is still processing. I've applied to almost every single retail management position I can find and have had several interviews with varying companies, but I keep getting passed over. I don't understand why my experience isn't good enough for jobs that are paying much less than what I was earning before. It's incredibly disheartening. My bank account is severely overdrawn, I can't afford to pay my bills, and I'm terrified that my car is going to get repossessed because I can't afford the monthly payments on my loan anymore. I can't afford any of the prescription medication I need either. My only saving grace is that my roommates are able to shoulder my portion of rent for now. I'm falling back into depression and I'm ashamed to ask my family for help because I feel like I'm always asking them for help. I feel like a failure, and it's absolutely awful. I am dying to go back to work. I hate sitting around the house doing nothing. I just don't understand why I'm not able to find somewhere that will hire me. I guess that's it. If you made it this far and read through my rant, thank you for providing a listening ear. I hope everyone has a fantastic holiday, whatever you celebrate, and a wonderful New Year. TLDR; I lost my job and feel like a failure. _________________ EDIT: A few people commented suggesting I post a link to my etsy or paypal. If you'd like to buy something from my etsy, feel free, but please don't feel obligated. The link is here: https://www.etsy.com/shop/InkyPawWorkshop My Paypal is here, but PLEASE don't feel like you have to donate at all. That wasn't the point of my posting this. I truly did just want to vent my frustrations. https://www.paypal.com/paypalme/sarahsan16
item.dc:creator: SarahSan16

item.title: Conan lore – the horrors of Dagon
item.description:
This is an account on the name of a city ruled by a godlike being in the original stories, and how it relates to real mythology, cosmic horror, and other Conan media.
The setting of Conan the barbarian is the so called Hyborian Age, a fictitious historical period set about 12,000 years ago (art by Earl Norem). The Age is polytheistic and its creator, Robert. E. Howard (1906 - 1936), often included elements from real historical myths and religions in reimagined form, such as Ymir, Ishtar, and Dagon.
The historical Dagon, or Dagan, was a father of gods, granted human kings authority in ancient Syria, and offered prosperity and abundant harvests. From the Bible is Dagon known as chief god of the Philistines. In medieval times Dagon was mistranslated as referring to fish, why in the 19th and 20th century he was mistaken for mythological half-fish beings; this outdated idea is still perpetuated by many churches.
Robert. E. Howard named locations in his stories after Dagon; but if there possibly are some water associations through geographic proximity there are none to fish. In the short story The Devil in Iron (1934) was Conan from Cimmeria (grey) at the Vilayet Sea (blue). At the southern end of Vilayet lay the Isle of Xapur (red).
In the jungle of Xapur lay the ruins of Dagonia, also called Dagon. It had been the capital city of the ancient empire of Dagonia, a civilization that ended long before the Hyborian Age. Here the barbarian arrived not long after a great evil had emerged from a tomb in the ruins of Dagon (art by Mandryk). Spoiler warning: for those who wish the read The Devil in Iron unspoiled, it is in the public domain https://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks06/0600801h.html
The evil being was named Khosatral Khel and had the apperance of a man, yet was greater in stature and what appeared to be flesh was living iron. With powerful magic Khosatral Khel restored the lost city, its huge walls, and even revived its dead citizens. Conan entered the citadel of Dagon where he overheared its master speak with an inhuman voice (art by John Buscema for Savage Sword of Conan 15).
The hypnotic voice of Khosatral had the resonance of a bell tolling at midnight: "`There was no life in the Abyss, save that which was incorporated in me,' it tolled. `Nor was there light, nor motion, nor any sound. Only the urge behind and beyond life guided and impelled me on my upward journey, blind, insensate, inexorable. Through ages upon ages, and the changeless strata of darkness I climbed—'" – The Devil in Iron; Chapter 5, onwards (art by ChrisCold)
The voice invoked in Conan a vision of it crawling up from Night and the Abyss ages ago, to clothe itself in the substances of the material universe (art by MrZarono): "But human flesh was too frail, too paltry to hold the terrific essence that was Khosatral Khel. So he stood up in the shape and aspect of a man, but his flesh was not flesh; nor the bone, bone; nor blood, blood. He became a blasphemy against all nature, for he caused to live and think and act a basic substance that before had never known the pulse and stir of animate being. He stalked through the world as a god, for no earthly weapon could harm him, and to him a century was like an hour."
"In his wanderings he came upon a primitive people inhabiting the island of Dagonia, and it pleased him to give this race culture and civilization, and by his aid they built the city of Dagon and they abode there and worshipped him. Strange and grisly were his servants, called from the dark corners of the planet where grim survivals of forgotten ages yet lurked. His house in Dagon was connected with every other house by tunnels through which his shaven-headed priests bore victims for the sacrifice" (art by StaceyAnnBorg).
In time a folk named Yuetshi settled nearby. The fierce Yuetshi were defeated in battle "and enslaved, and for nearly a generation they died on the altars of Khosatral. His sorcery kept them in bonds." Then a priest among the Yuetshi went into the wild and returned with "a knife that was of no earthly substance. It was forged of a meteor, which flashed through the sky like a flaming arrow and fell in a far valley. The slaves rose. Their saw-edged crescents cut down the men of Dagon like sheep, and against that unearthly knife the magic of Khosatral was impotent."
The priest overcame Khosatral in the citadel but slew not "his foe, because he wished to hold the threat of his loosing over the heads of his own rebellious subjects. He had left Khosatral lying upon the golden dais with the mystic knife across his breast for a spell to hold him senseless and inanimate until doomsday." But an ignorant looter had removed it at last and when Conan faced Khosatral he instantly knew that it was "the ancient from the Abyss, the god of Dagonia." Yet from where the names Dagonia and Dagon come from, or mean, is not explained in the tale (art by Boris Vallejo).
With the mystic knife Conan slew Khosatral but he could not stand to behold the metal body melt away in "an awful transmutation; in his dying throes Khosatral Khel had become again the thing that had crawled up from the Abyss millennia gone." With his death the pinnacles of Dagon faded like smoke—the battlements, the crenellated towers, the great bronze gates, the velvets, the gold, the ivory, and its women and men. Only the stumps of broken columns rose above crumbling walls and broken paves and shattered dome (art by Dennis Chan).
Robert E. Howard corresponded with the cosmic horror author H. P. Lovecraft (1890 - 1937), who was inspired by the fish-associated concept of Dagon when he used the name for one of his Great Old Ones, primordial and malignant beings of unfathomable power. It may been the creature that appears from the sea in the short story Dagon from 1919 (art by Alberto Dal Lago and Jacopo Schiavo): "Vast, Polyphemus-like, and loathsome, it darted like a stupendous monster of nightmares to the monolith, about which it flung its gigantic scaly arms, the while it bowed its hideous head and gave vent to certain measured sounds."
Polyphemus was a giant cyclops in Greek mythology, and a son of Poseidon. Still it is possible the creature in the short story is a huge speciment of the Deep Ones, a hideous fish-humanoid race serving Lovecraft's Dagon (art by Alejandro Mirabal). Lovecraft and authors he corresponded with loaned elements from each other at times. Howard also used Dagon, yet in his own manner - for despite attempts by Cthulhu Mythos fans to arrange works of Lovecraft and some associates into a single, coherent universe, that was not the intent of the authors.
Furthermore, while the authors payed homage to each other, Howard was not subject to Lovecraft's ideas and was himself very interested in history and mythology. Two years before The Devil in Iron Howard had released the pseudo-historical short story Worms of the Earth (1932). It is about a Pictish warrior king, Bran Mak Morn, in third century AD Britain. Though set in historical time it is in the same fictional universe as Conan (right: art by Gary Gianni).
Due to Bran Mak Morn's conflict with the Roman Empire he summons an ancient terror, the Worms [possibly meant to been spelled Wyrms but edited by the publisher; modern worm is from Old English wurm and wyrm, meaning "serpent, snake, dragon, reptile"]. The Worms were once humans but had transformed into horrible, reptile-looking creatures, and banished to live underground. They had magic powers and were apperently associated with Dagon since Bran on his quest visits the Dagon-moor, the old mound Dagon’s Barrow, the pool of Dagon’s Mere, and finally the ancient stone circle of Dagon’s Ring. Yet as in Conan's tale, who or what Dagon is is not told.
In the 1970s Marvel began its Conan comics in which appears a god named Dagon. He has a human form in lands where he is worshipped by humans (green); and is presented as the husband of Derketa, a goddess of pleasure and death - however the lands where peoples worship Derketa (red) are not known to worship Dagon. At other times Dagon appears as a great fish-monster. As such he is worshipped by beastly Mound Dwellers and by [Lovecraft's] Deep Ones, which sometimes are hostile to Dagon's human followers. Comic scholars have proposed that the incoherent apperances signifies different entities sharing the same name: http://www.marvunapp.com/Appendix/dagon.htm
In the 1984 film Conan the Destroyer the hero (Arnold Schwarzenegger) is on a quest on behalf of Queen Taramis (Sarah Douglas) to acquire a jeweled horn. This horn will awake the petrified god Dagoth. Is he related to Dagon? In a Conan short story by Howard from 1933 was told how some "pre-human ruins on Dagoth Hill" were home to a demon (The Scarlet Citadel; chapter 3).
The paperback of Conan the Swordsman (1978) has 'The Index of Hyborian Names' that attributes the hill's name as "probably from Dagon + Koth" [the latter is the country Koth, the lay above Shem]. The authority of the index is unknown to me, yet it has been proposed that the film's inspiration was a mix of Khosatral Khel and Dagon. When Dagoth (Andre the Giant) awakes from his stone sleep he proves to be a webbed monster. He is not easily defeated, – but neither is a barbarian from Cimmeria.
Public domain stories (art by Abrar Khan): The Devil in Iron https://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks06/0600801h.html Dagon https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Weird_Tales/Volume_2/Issue_3/Dagon Worms of the Earth https://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks06/0607861h.html
Some related accounts (photo: Wilt Chamberlain, Arnold Schwarzenegger, and Andre the Giant at the set to Conan the Destroyer): The Witch (film) https://imgur.com/gallery/s2zqUvD Salome/Taramis https://imgur.com/gallery/0G0Hp7r – – – – For further Conan lore posts, see https://imgur.com/gallery/Xc4OIH8

item.title: Hump Day, Dump Day
item.description:
He’s a treasure on land (a menace in the skies)
Children are incubators for plagues and given that mine was locked inside for his first 2 years, his immune system sucks.
Nice try, doctors
Us cynics need you!
Never played Starfield, I just think this would be a fun mechanic.
And he deserved it.
We love you, Toni Collette.
Intelligence is not Elon’s strength. That would be his money.
No, it’s big weepy baby disease and stupid bitch disease
You wouldn’t like Dark Gary.
Ooh, and I left the remote on the counter…
item.dc:creator: clevergrrl420
